name: Process Injection
summary: Malicious code was injected into a legitimate process (explorer.exe) so it runs under a trusted name and evades detection.

process.name: explorer.exe
process.why: explorer.exe should be a child of userinit.exe (not powershell.exe) and it holds RWX private memory - a hallmark of injection.

legit: A real explorer.exe is launched by userinit.exe at logon, runs at Medium integrity, has no RWX private memory and no unexpected outbound beacons.
parent_note: The parent powershell.exe is the injector. A normal explorer.exe has no live parent because userinit.exe exits after launching the shell.
modules_note: Look for an executable PRIVATE (unbacked) memory region and threads whose start address is not inside any loaded module - classic injected code.

PowerShell: Get-Process explorer | Select-Object Id, Path, StartTime

containment.q: What is the safest FIRST containment action?

options.feedback: Killing explorer.exe kills the shell and loses volatile in-memory evidence. Isolate and capture memory first.

options.text: Isolate the host from the network, then capture memory
options.feedback: Correct - isolation stops the beacon and preserves the injected code in a memory image before remediation.
A reboot destroys the injected in-memory code and the evidence.
The process is actively beaconing to C2 - inaction lets the attacker operate.

eradication: Terminate the injector (powershell.exe) and the injected thread
Remove any persistence the injector created
Run a full Microsoft Defender scan
recovery: Validate explorer.exe integrity and correct parentage
best_practices: Deploy Sysmon with a tuned config (log 8/10)
Enable Defender ASR rules and tamper protection
notes: Injected code lives in memory - preserve a memory image before killing.
Confirm with an RWX private region and an unbacked thread start address.

name: DLL Injection
summary: An unsigned DLL from a user-writable path was loaded into svchost.exe, which should only load signed system DLLs.

signals: Sysmon Event ID 7 (Image/DLL load) of an unsigned DLL

process.name: svchost.exe
process.why: svchost is a child of services.exe (correct), but it loaded an UNSIGNED DLL from C:\Users\Public - svchost should only load signed DLLs from System32.

legit: svchost loads only Microsoft-signed DLLs from C:\Windows\System32 and is always a child of services.exe.
parent_note: The parent services.exe is correct here - the anomaly is the loaded module, not the parent.
modules_note: Focus on Sysmon Event ID 7: check Signed=false and an ImageLoaded path outside System32.

event_logs.msg: Image Loaded: C:\Users\Public\evil.dll (Signed: false) into svchost.exe (PID 1020)

containment.q: How do you contain an unsigned DLL loaded into svchost?

options.feedback: Never - that destroys Windows. The bad module is evil.dll, not System32.

options.text: Isolate the host, then quarantine the malicious DLL and the process loading it
options.feedback: Correct - isolate, quarantine evil.dll, and stop the specific svchost group hosting it.
That breaks core services. Target only the affected instance.
Unsigned code in SYSTEM context must be remediated.

eradication: Remove the mechanism that loaded it (side-load / AppInit / phantom DLL)
best_practices: Monitor Sysmon Event ID 7 for unsigned loads
Remove write access to system paths
notes: Unsigned + non-System32 path is a strong indicator.
AppInit_DLLs and phantom-DLL side-loading are related vectors.

name: Process Hollowing
summary: notepad.exe was created suspended, its image replaced with malicious code, then resumed - it now beacons with no window.

signals: Sysmon 1: notepad.exe with unusual parent powershell.exe and no window
Image region is RWX and differs from notepad.exe on disk

process.name: notepad.exe
process.why: notepad.exe was hollowed - spawned by powershell, has no window, holds RWX image memory, and beacons to C2.

legit: notepad.exe is user-launched from explorer, has a visible window, and makes no network connections.
parent_note: powershell.exe spawning notepad.exe that then beacons is abnormal - notepad has no reason to be a child of PowerShell.
modules_note: The main image region is RWX and its bytes differ from notepad.exe on disk - the tell-tale sign of hollowing.

event_logs.msg: Process Create: notepad.exe, parent powershell.exe, no window

PowerShell: Get-Process notepad | Select-Object Id, Path, MainWindowTitle

containment.q: Best first step for a hollowed process beaconing to C2?

options.feedback: The on-disk file is untouched; the malicious code is in memory.

options.text: Isolate the host and capture memory, then terminate the process
options.feedback: Correct - preserve the in-memory payload, then kill it and the injector.
A reboot destroys the in-memory evidence.
notepad with no window and a network beacon is malicious.

eradication: Terminate the hollowed process and the injector
recovery: Validate notepad.exe on disk is untouched
notes: Compare the in-memory image to the on-disk file.
No window + network activity from notepad = red flag.

name: LSASS Credential Dumping
summary: A process read LSASS memory and wrote lsass.dmp to steal credentials (NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets).

signals: Sysmon 10: ProcessAccess to lsass with GrantedAccess 0x1410/0x1010

process.name: rundll32.exe
process.why: rundll32 is using the comsvcs.dll MiniDump LOLBIN to read lsass memory and write a dump - credential theft.

legit: lsass.exe is opened only by trusted OS components. A user process should NEVER read its memory.
parent_note: Focus on the accessing process (rundll32) and its command line rather than lsass's parentage.
modules_note: comsvcs.dll's MiniDump export is a living-off-the-land dumping technique - watch for it in command lines.

containment.q: Which is the correct immediate response?

options.feedback: Terminating/deleting lsass crashes Windows. Target the dumping process.

options.text: Isolate the host, kill the dumping process, and rotate exposed credentials
options.feedback: Correct - stop the dump, then rotate credentials because they may already be stolen.
A reboot alone doesn't rotate the exposed credentials.
Reading lsass is credential theft regardless of integrity level.

best_practices: Enable LSASS protection (RunAsPPL) and Credential Guard
notes: Any user process opening lsass with VM_READ is high-fidelity.
Containment isn't complete until exposed credentials are rotated.

name: Malicious Service Installation
summary: A new auto-start service masquerading as Windows Update runs an unsigned binary from ProgramData as SYSTEM.

signals: Auto-start service with a binary outside System32

process.name: WinUpd.exe
process.why: A new auto-start service ('WindowsUpdater') runs an unsigned binary from ProgramData as SYSTEM - real Windows Update runs from System32 via svchost.

legit: Real Windows Update runs as svchost (service wuauserv) from System32 and is signed by Microsoft.
parent_note: services.exe as parent is expected for a service - the anomaly is the unsigned ImagePath and the fake name.
modules_note: Check the service ImagePath and StartName; a generic 'Updater' name from ProgramData is suspicious.

event_logs.msg: A new service was installed: WindowsUpdater, ImagePath C:\ProgramData\WinUpd\WinUpd.exe

PowerShell: Get-Service WindowsUpdater | Select-Object Name, Status, StartType
Get-CimInstance Win32_Service -Filter "Name='WindowsUpdater'" | Select-Object Name, PathName, StartName

containment.q: Correct containment for a malicious auto-start service?

options.feedback: services.exe is the SCM - deleting it breaks Windows. Target the rogue service.

options.text: Stop and disable the service, then isolate the host
options.feedback: Correct - stop it to halt execution, then delete and clean up.
An auto-start service just relaunches on reboot.
The name is a masquerade; the ImagePath gives it away.

eradication: Remove the service registry key and binary
recovery: Confirm legitimate Windows Update still works
notes: ImagePath outside System32 + a generic 'Updater' name is suspicious.
Check StartName (LocalSystem) and StartType (Auto).

name: Service Hijacking
summary: A legitimate service ran from a user-writable folder; the attacker swapped its binary for a payload that runs as SYSTEM.

signals: FIM alert on a service binary change
Service binary hash mismatch / invalid signature

process.name: UpdaterSvc.exe
process.why: The service's install directory is writable by Users, so the binary was replaced with a payload that now runs as SYSTEM.

legit: The service binary should live in an Admin-only path with a valid Authenticode signature.
parent_note: services.exe parent is normal - the compromise is the swapped binary and the weak folder permissions.
modules_note: Check the binary's signature and the folder ACLs; a broken signature on a running service binary is a red flag.

containment.q: Best containment for a hijacked service binary?

options.text: Grant Users full control to fix it faster
options.feedback: That makes the weakness worse - Users should not write to service directories.

options.text: Stop the service, restore the signed binary, and fix the folder ACLs
options.feedback: Correct - remove the payload, restore the known-good binary, and remove the weak permissions.
The service restarts the payload on reboot.
Location doesn't matter if the folder is user-writable.

eradication: Replace with the known-good signed binary
Remove attacker write access with icacls
recovery: Restart the service and verify the signature
Audit other services for weak permissions and unquoted paths
best_practices: Audit service binary permissions and unquoted paths
notes: Check icacls for (M)/(W) granted to Users or Authenticated Users.
Unquoted service paths are a closely related weakness.

name: Registry Run-Key Persistence
summary: An autostart Run value was created to launch an unsigned binary from AppData at every logon.

signals: Autoruns flags an unknown Run entry
Defender alert on execution from AppData

process.name: payload.exe
process.why: A Run value ('Updater') launches an unsigned binary from AppData at every logon - user-level persistence.

legit: Legitimate Run entries point to signed vendor binaries in Program Files, not to AppData.
parent_note: explorer.exe launching the payload at logon is expected for a Run-key entry - the value itself is the persistence.
modules_note: Focus on the registry, not the process internals: which Run value points to AppData.

event_logs.msg: Process Create: payload.exe, parent explorer.exe, at logon

options.feedback: That would remove legitimate autostart entries too. Delete only the malicious value.

options.text: Isolate the host, terminate the payload, then remove the Run value
options.feedback: Correct - stop the running payload and delete just the malicious value.
The Run value re-launches the payload at the next logon.
An unsigned AppData autostart is malicious persistence.

eradication: Check RunOnce and other autostart locations
notes: Delete only the malicious value, not the whole key.
Check both HKLM and HKCU Run + RunOnce.

name: Winlogon Hijacking
summary: The Winlogon Userinit value was modified so an extra binary runs as SYSTEM at every logon.

signals: Sysmon Event ID 13 on Winlogon\Userinit or \Shell

process.name: evil.exe
process.why: Userinit was modified to also launch evil.exe, which winlogon runs at every logon as SYSTEM.

legit: The default Userinit is 'C:\Windows\system32\userinit.exe,' and Shell is 'explorer.exe' - nothing else.
parent_note: winlogon.exe spawning a binary from ProgramData is abnormal - winlogon should only start userinit.
modules_note: Compare the Winlogon Shell/Userinit values against their defaults.

containment.q: Correct remediation for a modified Userinit?

options.feedback: That prevents logon entirely. Fix the value, not the process.

options.text: Restore Userinit to the default value and remove the malicious binary
options.feedback: Correct - reset Userinit to the default and delete evil.exe.
The modified value re-runs evil.exe at the next logon.
SYSTEM-level logon persistence must be removed.

recovery: Verify logon works and Userinit is default
best_practices: Restrict write access to HKLM Winlogon
notes: Default Userinit ends with a trailing comma.
Shell should be exactly 'explorer.exe'.

name: Scheduled Task Persistence
summary: A scheduled task was created to run an unsigned binary at logon as SYSTEM.

process.name: payload.exe
process.why: A scheduled task ('\Microsoft\Windows\Updater') runs an unsigned binary as SYSTEM at logon.

legit: Legitimate tasks under \Microsoft\Windows\ run signed Microsoft binaries; a task pointing to ProgramData is suspicious.
parent_note: Tasks are launched by the Task Scheduler (svchost) - correct here; the task definition is the persistence.
modules_note: Enumerate non-Microsoft tasks and check the Actions (executable path).

containment.q: First step for a malicious scheduled task?

options.feedback: That breaks legitimate scheduling. Remove the specific task.

options.text: Disable/remove the task and terminate the running payload
options.feedback: Correct - unregister the task and kill the payload.
The task fires again on the next trigger.
A SYSTEM task running ProgramData binaries is malicious.

eradication: Delete the task XML and payload
best_practices: Alert on Security 4698 for non-standard task paths
Watch for hidden tasks (missing security descriptor)
notes: Attackers hide tasks by deleting the SD registry value.
Inspect C:\Windows\System32\Tasks directly.

name: WMI Event Persistence
summary: A permanent WMI event subscription runs a payload filelessly whenever a trigger fires.

process.name: scrcons.exe
process.why: scrcons.exe (the WMI script consumer host) is executing a permanent CommandLineEventConsumer payload - fileless WMI persistence.

legit: scrcons.exe normally does not run; a running instance driven by a CommandLineEventConsumer is suspicious.
parent_note: svchost (WMI) parent is expected; the persistence lives in the WMI repository, not the process.

event_logs.msg: WmiEvent Consumer created: CommandLineEventConsumer -> powershell payload

containment.q: How do you remove WMI event persistence?

options.feedback: WMI is core to Windows management. Remove the malicious subscription objects instead.

options.text: Delete the malicious __EventFilter, __EventConsumer and their binding in root\subscription
options.feedback: Correct - remove the filter, consumer, and binding to break the persistence.
The subscription survives reboots - it's stored in the CIM repository.
Disproportionate; targeted removal of the subscription is sufficient.

eradication: Remove the filter, consumer and binding
notes: WMI persistence is fileless - it lives in the CIM repository.
A running scrcons.exe is a strong indicator.
